name: Statistics for Data Science

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The eight courses form a coherent, deliberately cross-departmental data-science credential: a statistics spine (MATH 661 Applied Statistics, MATH 660 Statistical Computing with SAS/R, MATH 644 Regression, MATH 678 Statistical Methods in Data Science) is joined with computer-science courses (CS 636 Data Analytics with R, CS 644 Introduction to Big Data, CS 675 Machine Learning) and MATH 664 consulting. The blend maps cleanly onto the statistics-plus-computing skill set data science requires (deepestPrereqChain 1).
dimensions.suggestion: Add a recommended path pairing a statistics-computing foundation (MATH 660/661) with the ML and big-data courses so students sequence the CS and MATH pieces sensibly.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph has no dangling references (missingCourseCount 0) and an appropriately shallow depth of 1 for a certificate. The tradeoff is that several gates are stated as prose rather than course codes (CS 644 'permission of the instructor'; CS 675 'basic probability, linear algebra, computer programming'; CS 636 references topics of MATH 333/CS 280), so entry expectations are looser than the MATH courses.
dimensions.suggestion: Convert the CS course prerequisites from prose to explicit course codes or a stated skills checklist so certificate applicants can self-assess readiness.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No certificate-level learning outcomes appear in the data, leaving the intended data-science competencies (modeling, ML, big-data tooling) unstated.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish outcomes such as building and validating predictive models, programming analyses in R/SAS, and processing large datasets with big-data tools.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits and creditReconcileDelta are null, as expected for a certificate; the eight 3-credit courses imply a focused credential, but the required count and the intended MATH/CS mix are not stated.
dimensions.suggestion: State how many courses/credits are required and whether a minimum number must come from each of the statistics and computer-science groups.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Descriptions are present and informative, though a couple of cross-listed CS excerpts begin mid-sentence with prerequisite text (e.g., CS 636, CS 675), and the page lacks a stated credit total and required/elective structure.
dimensions.suggestion: Clean the cross-listed CS descriptions so they lead with the course content, and add a header stating required credits and group structure.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: This is the most current of the department's statistics certificates: it directly includes machine learning (CS 675), big data (CS 644), R analytics (CS 636), SAS/R computing (MATH 660), and a dedicated data-science methods course (MATH 678), matching mainstream data-science hiring. It stops short of deep learning, data engineering/pipelines, and deployment/MLOps.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a deep-learning or data-engineering elective so completers reach beyond introductory ML and batch big-data processing.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are specific and skills-oriented (CS 644 spanning data generation, storage, and analytics; MATH 660 detailing SAS/R data steps and merges; MATH 678 on model building for data science), though a few cross-listed CS excerpts carry leading prerequisite text.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are internally consistent with no dangling references, but the CS courses state gates as prose or 'permission of the instructor' rather than course codes, making entry requirements less precise than the MATH 644/664/678 gates on MATH 661.

missingTopics.rationale: CS 675 is an introductory machine-learning course; there is no deep-learning course despite its centrality to modern data science.
CS 644 introduces big data but no course covers relational databases, ETL, or production data pipelines that data scientists depend on.

missingTopics.topic: Unstructured data: NLP and text mining
missingTopics.rationale: The certificate is model- and tabular-data focused; text/NLP methods are a large and growing part of applied data science.
The statistics courses are frequentist; probabilistic/Bayesian modeling is increasingly used in data-science practice.
No dedicated visualization/communication course appears, yet conveying results is a core data-science competency beyond the general MATH 664 consulting course.

emergingTrends.rationale: Generative models and LLM tooling are reshaping data science; the ML pathway (CS 675) should be extended to cover them.
Productionizing, monitoring, and versioning models is now expected of data scientists but is absent from the certificate.
CS 644's big-data content should connect to cloud (Spark, cloud data warehouses) that dominate current practice.
Fairness, bias, and privacy are increasingly required competencies that the ML and data-science methods courses should address.
Beyond prediction, decision-making applications need causal methods that complement MATH 644/678.

topSuggestions: State required credits/courses (and any MATH-versus-CS distribution) and publish certificate learning outcomes.
Add a deep-learning or data-engineering/MLOps elective to advance beyond introductory ML and batch big data.
Convert the CS prerequisites from prose to explicit course codes or a skills checklist.
Clean the cross-listed CS course descriptions so they lead with content, not prerequisite text.
Introduce responsible-AI and causal-inference content to round out modern data-science practice.
